revision.text: {{draft}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a significant festival of [[Ise Jingu]]

{{draft}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a significant festival of [[Ise Jingu]] ==Annual festivals== [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 06.jpg|thumb|left|The Otaue ceremony.]] From the late seventh century, when the festivals and offerings of Ise Shrine became more formalised, a number of annual events have been performed at both Naikū and Gekū. The Tsukinamisai, which was held in June and December, as well as the Kannamesai Festival in September, were the only three offerings performed by the [[Saiō]], an imperial princess who served as high priestess of the shrine until the 14th century.<ref>Saikū Historical Museum information booklet, "A Town of Bamboo Illumined Once Again".</ref> These offerings are based on the cycle of the agricultural year and are still performed today.
The first important ceremony of the modern calendar year is the Kinensai, where prayers are offered for a bountiful harvest. Kazahinomisai, where prayers for fair weather and sufficient rains are made, is held twice a year in May and August at both Naikū and Gekū. [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]] The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |title=Isejingu |access-date=2007-10-10 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 }}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
Besides the agricultural ceremonies already mentioned, ceremonies and festivals are held throughout the year at both Naikū and Gekū to celebrate things such as the new year, the foundation of Japan, the past emperors, purification rituals for priests and court musicians, good sake fermentation and the [[The Emperor's Birthday|Emperor's birthday]]. There are also daily food offerings to the shrine kami held both in the mornings and evenings.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi2.htm |title=Isejingu |access-date=2007-10-10 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.04-182958/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi2.htm |archive-date=2012-08-04 }}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>

The Tsukinamisai, which was held in June and December, as well as the Kannamesai Festival in September, were the only three offerings performed by the [[Saiō]], an imperial princess who served as high priestess of the shrine until the 14th century.<ref>Saikū Historical Museum information booklet, "A Town of Bamboo Illumined Once Again".</ref> These offerings are based on the cycle of the agricultural year and are still performed today.

Kazahinomisai, where prayers for fair weather and sufficient rains are made, is held twice a year in May and August at both Naikū and Gekū. [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]] The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |title=Isejingu |access-date=2007-10-10 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 }}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>

There are also daily food offerings to the shrine kami held both in the mornings and evenings.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi2.htm |title=Isejingu |access-date=2007-10-10 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.04-182958/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi2.htm |archive-date=2012-08-04 }}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
[[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]]

==Annual festivals== [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 06.jpg|thumb|left|The Otaue ceremony.]] From the late seventh century, when the festivals and offerings of Ise Shrine became more formalised, a number of annual events have been performed at both Naikū and Gekū. [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]] The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. {{draft}} [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]] Kannamesai is a large festival at the Ise Shrines. It is known as Ōmatsuri in Ise. The festival honors Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess. It is a time to offer newly-harvested rice to her.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}{{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭[[ja:神嘗祭]]}} is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]]. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is a key seasonal event. It is one of the three main festivals of the year. Historical records describe it in different ways. It has been a major event since the early 20th century.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before Niinamesai at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Festivities == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The festival has ancient roots. It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] }}

{{draft}} [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}{{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭[[ja:神嘗祭]]}} sometimes called Ōmatsuri<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]]. The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is a key seasonal event. It is one of the three main festivals of the year. Historical records describe it in different ways. It has been a major event since the early 20th century.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before Niinamesai at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />

While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />

{{draft}} [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}{{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭[[ja:神嘗祭]]}} sometimes called Ōmatsuri<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]]. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
The most important annual festival held at Ise Shrine is the {{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭}}. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu. An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0" />
It is also celebrated at other shrines<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]]. Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is a key seasonal event. It is one of the three main festivals of the year. Historical records describe it in different ways. It has been a major event since the early 20th century.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before Niinamesai at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Festivities == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The festival has ancient roots. It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] }}

The festival happens before Niinamesai at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Festivities == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The festival has ancient roots. It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{draft}} [[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}{{nihongo|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}|神嘗祭[[ja:神嘗祭]]}} sometimes called Ōmatsuri<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at other shrines<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is a key seasonal event. It is one of the three main festivals of the year. Historical records describe it in different ways. It has been a major event since the early 20th century.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before Niinamesai at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the Kashikodokoro in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the Kashikodokoro together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
== Festivities == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The Kashikodokoro enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] }}

This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/2012.08.03-191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]]

The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Kannamesai Festival]]

In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
