{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}

A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Japanese Imperial Rituals}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|12=draft|12=draft}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}

The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. '''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11255045]] Wikidata name: Kanname-no-Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11255045]] Wikidata name: Kanname-no-Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}
[[en:Kannamesai Festival]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:神嘗祭]] [[ko:신상제 (10월)]] {{wikidata link|Q11255045}} [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[Category:October observances]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannamesai Festival]][[ja:神嘗祭]][[ko:신상제 (10월)]]
{{refimprove|date=November 2023}} {{Short description|Japanese festival}}
[[File:Bugaku(Naiku) 03.JPG|thumb|Autumn [[Kagura]] Festival.]]{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}}
'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />

'''Kannamesai Festival''', sometimes called '''Ōmatsuri'''<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai (Grand Shrines of Ise) |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9064 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> is one of the most important festivals of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /> It is also celebrated at all Shinto shrines according to regulations by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref name=":Kokugakuin">{{Cite web |last=Nakanishi |first=Masayuki |title=Kannamesai |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9118 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref> Held in October each year, this ritual makes offerings of the first harvest of crops for the season to Amaterasu.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> An imperial envoy carries the offering of rice harvested by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} himself to Ise, as well as five-coloured silk cloth and other materials, called ''heihaku.''<ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Isejingu |url=http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803191411/http://www.isejingu.or.jp/english/maturi/maturi5.htm |archive-date=2012-08-03 |access-date=2007-10-10}}{{Snd}} Annual Cycle of Ceremonies and Rice, Official Ise Jingu homepage.</ref>
The festival is part of a yearly cycle. The cycle starts with Shinden geshusai in April. This marks the beginning of rice planting. Otaue hajime follows in May and nuibosai in September. The cycle ends with the Kannamesai.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Kannamesai is one of the three main festivals of the year.<ref name=":1" />
The festival happens before [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] at the imperial palace. It follows the lunar calendar, taking place in the ninth month. An imperial envoy offers silk and other gifts.<ref name=":1" />
== Rituals and Ceremonies == The festival begins on the evening of October 15 with the Okitama shinsai and Miura no gi rituals. While the Outer Shrine presents offerings of yuki no ōmike, the Inner Shrine's offerings include sliced abalone dressed in salt (minie). An imperial envoy recites a prayer (saimon) and presents imperial offerings on the day commemorating Amaterasu Ōmikami's enshrinement, followed by a votive kagura performance by shrine musicians and dancers.<ref name=":1" />
The festival has two main ceremonies. These are "distant worship" (yohai) and a rite for imperial ancestors (''shinsai no gi)''. They take place at the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} in the imperial palace. A 1933 rule states the importance of Kannamesai for Ise Shrines.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
A rule says rituals must happen at Ise Shrines and the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} together. This made Kannamesai a major palace ritual. In 1914, all central and lesser shrine priests had to include Kannamesai. After the war, Kannamesai was named a medium-scale festival. All shrines must observe it.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" /> == History == The tradition began in 721. Imperial messengers brought offerings in the ninth month. The offerings were called "reihei". The practice stopped in medieval times. It started again in the Edo period.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />
It dates back to Emperor Suinin's time. Legend says Princess {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} looked for a place to honor the goddess. A white-naped crane offered rice ears, starting this tradition.<ref name=":1" />
In 1871, the festival rules changed. The festival then included worship at Kōtai Jingū and the imperial palace. The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror. This mirror represents the Ise Shrines in the palace. The festival moved to October in 1878. This was to match the harvest time.<ref name=":Kokugakuin" />Japan started using the Gregorian calendar in 1873. Kannamesai moved to the tenth month in 1879. This change matched the rice harvest season.<ref name=":1" />
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist}}

The {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|1=Kashiko-dokoro|2=ja|3=賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro|qq=}} enshrines a sacred mirror.
